name: List Data Structures
description: The core concepts behind lists, stacks, and queues — essential building blocks of computer science.

group.definition: A flexible data structure that stores elements either contiguously (an **Array**) or non-contiguously (a **Linked List**), accessed by index or reference.
A contiguous block of memory holding elements of the same type, offering fast O(1) **Index** access but a **Static** size.
A **List** whose elements (**Node**s) are linked by references, allowing easy insertion and removal without contiguous memory.
A single unit in a **Linked List** holding a value and a reference to the next element.
The numeric position used to access an element directly within an **Array** or array-based **List**.
A **List** that follows the **LIFO** principle, where the last element added is the first removed; used to manage function **Call Stack**s.
Last-In-First-Out — the ordering rule of a **Stack**, where the most recently added element leaves first.
The operation that adds an element to the top of a **Stack**.
The operation that removes the top element from a **Stack**, the counterpart of **Push**.
A **Stack** used by programs to track active function calls and their local variables.
A **List** that follows the **FIFO** principle, where the first element added is the first removed; ideal for processing tasks in order.
First-In-First-Out — the ordering rule of a **Queue**, like people waiting in a line.
The operation that adds an element to the back of a **Queue**.
The operation that removes an element from the front of a **Queue**, the counterpart of **Enqueue**.
A double-ended **Queue** allowing efficient insertion and removal at both the front and the rear, so it can serve as either a **Stack** (**LIFO**) or a **Queue** (**FIFO**).
A fixed-size structure, like a plain **Array**, whose capacity is set at creation and cannot change.
A resizable structure, like a dynamic **List**, that can grow or shrink during program execution unlike a **Static** array.
The first **Node** of a **Linked List**, the entry point from which traversal begins.
The last **Node** of a **Linked List**; in a **Singly Linked List** its next reference is null.
A **Linked List** where each **Node** points only to the next one, so it can be traversed in a single direction and reaching an **Index** takes O(n).
A **Linked List** whose **Node**s store references to both the next and previous elements, enabling bidirectional traversal and O(1) deletion of a known node.
A **Linked List** whose **Tail** links back to the **Head**, forming a continuous loop; well suited to cyclical processes like **Round-Robin Scheduling**.
A CPU scheduling strategy that cycles through processes in fixed time slices, naturally modeled with a **Circular Linked List**.
A **List** that stores elements of a single shared data type, like a typed **Array**.
A **List** that holds elements of different data types at once, often implemented by storing references to a common base class or interface.
